figures.caption: Table 3: Reliability diagnostics for multi-step causal influence. Panel A averages diagnostics over corridor, 5m_vs_6m, and MMM2. Panel B corrupts the forward model on 5m_vs_6m with H = 3.

Figure 1: Illustrative cooperative predator–prey scenario with two predators a and b chasing a prey. Panel (a) shows a delayed but beneficial give-way action by a; panel (b) shows a high-influence action by a that harms team return.
Table 5: MPE training hyperparameters for MADDPG-based experiments.

Figure 4: Team episodic return vs. environment steps on the Cooperative Predator-Prey task for MAPPO and MAPPO+MAGIC. When plugged into a different CTDE backbone, MAGIC again improves both convergence speed and final performance, illustrating its plug-and-play compatibility.

Table 2: Component analysis across MPE and SMAC. Panel A reports final and best returns on MPE Predator Prey. Panel B reports final Win% on representative SMAC maps. Standard deviations and full MPE scalar metrics are provided in Appendix D.3.
Table 12: Full horizon sensitivity and rollout-error diagnostics.
Figure 5: Forward-model diagnostics on Cooperative Predator-Prey. We report the mean-squared error of predicted joint observations as a function of rollout horizon h.

Figure 2: Overview of MAGIC. A CTDE backbone collects trajectories and updates policies during training. The MAGIC intrinsic-reward module estimates multi-step causal influence from learned-dynamics intervention rollouts using an ICMI critic. An extrinsic value function estimates team advantage, which gates the raw influence score before it is used as intrinsic reward. The forward model, ICMI critic, and gate are used only during training, while execution uses the learned decentralized policies.
Figure 3: Learning curves on MPE tasks over five random seeds. Shaded regions denote standard deviation across seeds.
